questions.question: Why did pre-OneVision open VLMs split into single-image, multi-image, and video lineages?
options: Licensing prevented combining them
Each scenario stresses a different budget: single-image wants ~2880 high-res tokens, multi-image wants several at ~576, video wants many frames at ~196 after pooling — separate models each picked one
Video requires a different programming language
The benchmarks forbade general models
questions.explanation: Each lineage won its benchmark and failed the others. LLaVA-OneVision's bet: one model with a unified budget and the right curriculum beats the sum of specialists at equal parameters.

questions.question: What is the OneVision unified token budget trick?
options: Always exactly 1000 tokens
Roughly constant ~3000-4000 visual tokens per sample, allocated differently per scenario — AnyRes tiles with pooling for single images, 729/image for multi-image, 32 frames at 81 tokens for video
Unlimited tokens with a bigger context
One token per image regardless of content
questions.explanation: The encoder produces different geometry per scenario, but the LLM consumes a constant budget — heterogeneous inputs never blow the context.

questions.question: What did the curriculum-order ablation show?
options: Order doesn't matter with enough data
Single-image-first (perception base), then mixed OneVision, then task transfer — video-first or multi-image-first produces worse image performance even with identical data
Video must always come first
Each stage needs a weight reset
questions.explanation: Training all scenarios from scratch together underfits perception and overfits structure — a model that follows cross-image reasoning patterns but is visually shallow. Stage SI builds the base the later stages compose on.

questions.question: What is the "task transfer" argument for one model over specialists?
options: One model is cheaper to host, nothing more

Emergent cross-scenario effects: single-image skills export to video, multi-image reasoning exports back to single-image — capabilities the specialists never showed, like multi-camera reasoning and screenshot-agent behavior
Specialists cannot be benchmarked
Transfer learning only works on text
questions.explanation: The reported emergent trio — multi-camera reasoning, set-of-mark prompting, iPhone-screenshot agent — came from skills crossing scenario boundaries, beating the sum of separate lineages.

questions.question: How does OneVision fit 32 video frames into the budget?
options: By dropping every other frame's colors
Aggressive 3x3 bilinear pooling reduces each 384px frame to 81 tokens — 32 × 81 = 2,592 tokens, within the unified budget
By converting video to a text transcript
Frames share one token via averaging
questions.explanation: Temporal coverage trades against per-frame resolution: many low-token frames for dynamics, versus few high-token tiles for OCR-grade detail in the single-image allocation.
